questions.question: Production data shows that in 12% of cases, your agent skips get_customer entirely and calls lookup_order using only the customer's stated name, occasionally leading to misidentified accounts and incorrect refunds. What change would most effectively address this reliability issue?

options.A: Add a programmatic prerequisite that blocks lookup_order and process_refund calls until get_customer has returned a verified customer ID.
options.B: Enhance the system prompt to state that customer verification via get_customer is mandatory before any order operations.
options.C: Add few-shot examples showing the agent always calling get_customer first, even when customers volunteer order details.
options.D: Implement a routing classifier that analyzes each request and enables only the subset of tools appropriate for that request type.

questions.explanation: When a specific tool sequence is required for critical business logic (like verifying customer identity before processing refunds), programmatic enforcement provides deterministic guarantees that prompt-based approaches cannot. Options B and C rely on probabilistic LLM compliance, which is insufficient when errors have financial consequences. Option D addresses tool availability rather than tool ordering, which is not the actual problem.

questions.question: Production logs show the agent frequently calls get_customer when users ask about orders (e.g., 'check my order #12345'), instead of calling lookup_order. Both tools have minimal descriptions ('Retrieves customer information' / 'Retrieves order details') and accept similar identifier formats. What's the most effective first step to improve tool selection reliability?

options.A: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct tool selection patterns, with 5-8 examples showing order-related queries routing to lookup_order.
options.B: Expand each tool's description to include input formats it handles, example queries, edge cases, and boundaries explaining when to use it versus similar tools.
options.C: Implement a routing layer that parses user input before each turn and pre-selects the appropriate tool based on detected keywords and identifier patterns.
options.D: Consolidate both tools into a single lookup_entity tool that accepts any identifier and internally determines which backend to query.

questions.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection. When descriptions are minimal, models lack the context to differentiate between similar tools. Option B directly addresses this root cause with a low-effort, high-leverage fix. Few-shot examples (A) add token overhead without fixing the underlying issue. A routing layer (C) is over-engineered and bypasses the LLM's natural language understanding. Consolidating tools (D) is a valid architectural choice but requires more effort than a 'first step' warrants.

questions.question: Your agent achieves 55% first-contact resolution, well below the 80% target. Logs show it escalates straightforward cases (standard damage replacements with photo evidence) while attempting to autonomously handle complex situations requiring policy exceptions. What's the most effective way to improve escalation calibration?

options.A: Add explicit escalation criteria to your system prompt with few-shot examples demonstrating when to escalate versus resolve autonomously.
options.B: Have the agent self-report a confidence score (1-10) before each response and automatically route requests to humans when confidence falls below a threshold.
options.C: Deploy a separate classifier model trained on historical tickets to predict which requests need escalation before the main agent begins processing.
options.D: Implement sentiment analysis to detect customer frustration levels and automatically escalate when negative sentiment exceeds a threshold.

questions.explanation: Adding explicit escalation criteria with few-shot examples directly addresses the root cause: unclear decision boundaries. This is the proportionate first response before adding infrastructure. Option B fails because LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated. Option C is over-engineered, requiring labeled data and ML infrastructure when prompt optimization hasn't been tried. Option D solves a different problem; sentiment doesn't correlate with case complexity.

questions.question: You want to create a custom /review slash command that runs your team's standard code review checklist. This command should be available to every developer when they clone or pull the repository. Where should you create this command file?

options.A: In the .claude/commands/ directory in the project repository
options.B: In ~/.claude/commands/ in each developer's home directory
options.C: In the CLAUDE.md file at the project root
options.D: In a .claude/config.json file with a commands array

questions.explanation: Project-scoped custom slash commands should be stored in the .claude/commands/ directory within the repository. These commands are version-controlled and automatically available to all developers. Option B is for personal commands not shared via version control. Option C is for project instructions, not command definitions. Option D describes a configuration mechanism that doesn't exist in Claude Code.

questions.question: You've been assigned to restructure the team's monolithic application into microservices. This will involve changes across dozens of files and requires decisions about service boundaries and module dependencies. Which approach should you take?

options.A: Enter plan mode to explore the codebase, understand dependencies, and design an implementation approach before making changes.
options.B: Start with direct execution and make changes incrementally, letting the implementation reveal the natural service boundaries.
options.C: Use direct execution with comprehensive upfront instructions detailing exactly how each service should be structured.
options.D: Begin in direct execution mode and only switch to plan mode if you encounter unexpected complexity during implementation.

questions.explanation: Plan mode is designed for complex tasks involving large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, and architectural decisions. It enables safe codebase exploration and design before committing to changes. Option B risks costly rework when dependencies are discovered late. Option C assumes you already know the right structure without exploring the code. Option D ignores that the complexity is already stated in the requirements.

questions.question: Your codebase has distinct areas with different coding conventions: React components use functional style with hooks, API handlers use async/await with specific error handling, and database models follow a repository pattern. Test files are spread throughout the codebase alongside the code they test. What's the most maintainable way to ensure Claude automatically applies the correct conventions when generating code?

options.A: Create rule files in .claude/rules/ with YAML frontmatter specifying glob patterns to conditionally apply conventions based on file paths
options.B: Consolidate all conventions in the root CLAUDE.md file under headers for each area, relying on Claude to infer which section applies
options.C: Create skills in .claude/skills/ for each code type that include the relevant conventions in their SKILL.md files
options.D: Place a separate CLAUDE.md file in each subdirectory containing that area's specific conventions

questions.explanation: .claude/rules/ with glob patterns (e.g., **/*.test.tsx) allows conventions to be automatically applied based on file paths regardless of directory location — essential for test files spread throughout the codebase. Option B relies on inference rather than explicit matching. Option C requires manual skill invocation. Option D can't easily handle files spread across many directories since CLAUDE.md files are directory-bound.

questions.question: After running the system on the topic 'impact of AI on creative industries,' you observe that each subagent completes successfully but the final reports cover only visual arts, completely missing music, writing, and film production. The coordinator's logs show it decomposed the topic into three subtasks: 'AI in digital art creation,' 'AI in graphic design,' and 'AI in photography.' What is the most likely root cause?

options.A: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps in the findings it receives from other agents.
options.B: The coordinator agent's task decomposition is too narrow, resulting in subagent assignments that don't cover all relevant domains of the topic.
options.C: The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough and need to be expanded to cover more creative industry sectors.
options.D: The document analysis agent is filtering out sources related to non-visual creative industries due to overly restrictive relevance criteria.

questions.explanation: The coordinator's logs reveal the root cause directly: it decomposed 'creative industries' into only visual arts subtasks. The subagents executed their assigned tasks correctly — the problem is what they were assigned. Options A, C, and D incorrectly blame downstream agents that are working correctly within their assigned scope.

questions.question: The web search subagent times out while researching a complex topic. You need to design how this failure information flows back to the coordinator agent. Which error propagation approach best enables intelligent recovery?

options.A: Return structured error context to the coordinator including the failure type, the attempted query, any partial results, and potential alternative approaches.
options.B: Implement automatic retry logic with exponential backoff within the subagent, returning a generic 'search unavailable' status only after all retries are exhausted.
options.C: Catch the timeout within the subagent and return an empty result set marked as successful.
options.D: Propagate the timeout exception directly to a top-level handler that terminates the entire research workflow.

questions.explanation: Structured error context gives the coordinator the information it needs to make intelligent recovery decisions. Option B's generic status hides valuable context. Option C suppresses the error by marking failure as success. Option D terminates the entire workflow unnecessarily when recovery strategies could succeed.

questions.question: During testing, you observe that the synthesis agent frequently needs to verify specific claims while combining findings. Currently, when verification is needed, the synthesis agent returns control to the coordinator, adding 2-3 round trips per task and increasing latency by 40%. 85% of these verifications are simple fact-checks while 15% require deeper investigation. What's the most effective approach?

options.A: Give the synthesis agent a scoped verify_fact tool for simple lookups, while complex verifications continue delegating to the web search agent through the coordinator.
options.B: Have the synthesis agent accumulate all verification needs and return them as a batch to the coordinator at the end of its pass.
options.C: Give the synthesis agent access to all web search tools so it can handle any verification need directly without round-trips through the coordinator.
options.D: Have the web search agent proactively cache extra context around each source during initial research, anticipating what the synthesis agent might need to verify.

questions.explanation: Option A applies the principle of least privilege by giving the synthesis agent only what it needs for the 85% common case while preserving the existing coordination pattern for complex cases. Option B creates blocking dependencies since synthesis steps may depend on earlier verified facts. Option C over-provisions the synthesis agent, violating separation of concerns. Option D relies on speculative caching.

questions.question: Your pipeline script runs `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues"` but the job hangs indefinitely. Logs indicate Claude Code is waiting for interactive input. What's the correct approach to run Claude Code in an automated pipeline?

options.A: Add the -p flag: `claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`
options.B: Set the environment variable CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true before running the command
options.C: Redirect stdin from /dev/null: `claude "Analyze..." < /dev/null`
options.D: Add the --batch flag: `claude --batch "Analyze..."`

questions.explanation: The -p (or --print) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode. It processes the prompt, outputs the result to stdout, and exits without waiting for user input. The other options reference non-existent features (CLAUDE_HEADLESS, --batch) or use Unix workarounds that don't properly address Claude Code's command syntax.

questions.question: Your team wants to reduce API costs. Currently, real-time Claude calls power two workflows: (1) a blocking pre-merge check that must complete before developers can merge, and (2) a technical debt report generated overnight. Your manager proposes switching both to the Message Batches API for its 50% cost savings. How should you evaluate this proposal?

options.A: Use batch processing for the technical debt reports only; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks.
options.B: Switch both workflows to batch processing with status polling to check for completion.
options.C: Keep real-time calls for both workflows to avoid batch result ordering issues.
options.D: Switch both to batch processing with a timeout fallback to real-time if batches take too long.

questions.explanation: The Message Batches API offers 50% cost savings but has processing times up to 24 hours with no guaranteed latency SLA. This makes it unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks but ideal for overnight batch jobs. Option B relies on 'often faster' completion which isn't acceptable for blocking workflows. Option C ignores valid cost optimization. Option D adds unnecessary complexity.

questions.question: A pull request modifies 14 files across the stock tracking module. Your single-pass review produces inconsistent results: detailed feedback for some files, superficial comments for others, obvious bugs missed, and contradictory feedback — flagging a pattern as problematic in one file while approving identical code elsewhere. How should you restructure the review?

options.A: Split into focused passes: analyze each file individually for local issues, then run a separate integration-focused pass examining cross-file data flow.
options.B: Require developers to split large PRs into smaller submissions of 3-4 files before the automated review runs.
options.C: Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window to give all 14 files adequate attention in one pass.
options.D: Run three independent review passes on the full PR and only flag issues that appear in at least two of the three runs.

questions.explanation: Splitting reviews into focused passes directly addresses the root cause: attention dilution when processing many files at once. File-by-file analysis ensures consistent depth, while a separate integration pass catches cross-file issues. Option B shifts burden to developers. Option C misunderstands that larger context windows don't solve attention quality issues. Option D would suppress detection of real bugs by requiring consensus.

questions.question: Your agentic loop occasionally continues running after Claude has finished its task. The code checks `if len(response.content) > 0 and response.content[0].type == "text"` to determine completion. What is wrong with this approach?

options.A: The code should check for response.content[0].type == "tool_result" instead.
options.B: The code should count the number of iterations and stop after a fixed maximum.
options.C: Claude can return text alongside tool_use blocks in the same response; checking content type does not reliably indicate completion. The code should check stop_reason instead.
options.D: The code should parse the text content for phrases like "I'm done" or "task complete."

questions.explanation: Text presence does not mean the agent is finished — Claude often returns explanatory text before tool calls. The stop_reason field ('end_turn' vs 'tool_use') is the only deterministic signal for whether the agent intends to continue working.

questions.question: In a multi-agent research system, the synthesis subagent produces a report missing key findings that the web search subagent discovered. Logs show the coordinator invoked the synthesis agent after the web search completed. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: The synthesis agent's system prompt does not include instructions to incorporate all findings.
options.B: The coordinator did not explicitly pass the web search results in the synthesis subagent's prompt — subagents do not inherit the coordinator's conversation history.
options.C: The web search agent stored its results in a shared database that the synthesis agent could not access.
options.D: The synthesis agent's tool set does not include a tool for reading previous agent outputs.

questions.explanation: Subagent context isolation means the synthesis agent starts with only what the coordinator provides in its prompt. There is no automatic inheritance of prior results. The coordinator must explicitly pass the web search findings as part of the synthesis subagent's input.

questions.question: A customer support agent needs to verify customer identity before processing any refunds. Currently, the system prompt says 'Always verify the customer before processing refunds.' Production logs show the agent skips verification in 8% of cases. What should you do?

options.A: Implement a programmatic hook (PreToolUse) that blocks the process_refund tool unless get_customer has already been called and returned a verified ID.
options.B: Add more emphatic language to the system prompt: 'You MUST ALWAYS verify first. NEVER skip this step.'
options.C: Add 10 few-shot examples showing the correct verification-first sequence.
options.D: Reduce the agent's temperature to make it more deterministic.

questions.explanation: When business rules require guaranteed compliance, programmatic enforcement (hooks) is the only approach with zero failure rate. Prompt-based approaches are probabilistic — no matter how emphatic the wording, they cannot guarantee 100% adherence.

questions.question: An agent has access to 15 tools and frequently selects the wrong one for user requests. What is the most effective architectural change?

options.A: Write longer, more detailed descriptions for all 15 tools.
options.B: Add few-shot examples showing correct tool selection for common queries.
options.C: Implement a pre-processing classifier that selects the tool before the agent runs.
options.D: Reduce the agent's tool set to 4-5 core tools and distribute the rest across specialized subagents.

questions.explanation: Tool selection reliability degrades significantly above 5 tools. The architectural solution is distributing tools across specialized subagents, not writing better descriptions for 15 tools or adding a non-LLM classifier that loses context awareness.

questions.question: Your MCP tool returns `{"status": "error", "message": "Operation failed"}` when it cannot connect to the database. The agent keeps retrying indefinitely. What is wrong with the error response?

options.A: The error message should include the database connection string for debugging.
options.B: The error should be thrown as an exception rather than returned as a result.
options.C: The response lacks structured metadata (isError, errorCategory, isRetryable). Without knowing whether the error is transient or permanent, the agent cannot make intelligent recovery decisions.
options.D: The tool should silently return an empty result set instead of an error.

questions.explanation: Structured error responses with errorCategory and isRetryable flags let the agent distinguish transient failures (worth retrying) from permanent ones (escalate or try an alternative approach). Without this metadata, the agent defaults to retrying blindly.

questions.question: You need to configure an MCP server that requires an API key for your project team. Where should the API key go?

options.A: Directly in the .mcp.json file as a string value.
options.B: In an environment variable, referenced in .mcp.json using ${API_KEY} expansion syntax.
options.C: In the project-level CLAUDE.md file.
options.D: In each developer's ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md with instructions to set the key.

questions.explanation: Environment variable expansion (${VAR}) in .mcp.json keeps secrets out of version-controlled files. Hardcoding keys in config files is a critical security anti-pattern, and storing them in CLAUDE.md exposes them in plaintext.

questions.question: A new team member joins and reports that Claude Code is not following the team's coding standards. Other team members have no issues. What is the most likely configuration problem?

options.A: The team's standards are defined in a skill that the new member hasn't invoked yet.
options.B: The team's standards are in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (user-level) on existing members' machines instead of the project-level .claude/CLAUDE.md that would be shared via version control.
options.C: The new member has not installed the required MCP servers.
options.D: The new member needs to run /memory to load the team's configuration files.

questions.explanation: User-level CLAUDE.md only exists on each individual's machine. Team standards must be in project-level config (.claude/CLAUDE.md or .claude/rules/) to be shared via version control and automatically available to all team members.

questions.question: You want to create a codebase analysis skill that produces verbose output and explores multiple files. How should you configure it to avoid polluting the main conversation context?

options.A: Create a skill in .claude/skills/ with context: fork in the SKILL.md frontmatter.
options.B: Create a slash command in .claude/commands/ with a note to clean up after execution.
options.C: Put the analysis instructions in a subdirectory CLAUDE.md file.
options.D: Use plan mode for the analysis to keep it separate from implementation.

questions.explanation: context: fork runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent context. Its exploration output stays separate from the main session. Commands run in the current session and would pollute context with all the verbose output.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline runs `claude "Review this PR for security issues"` and it hangs. What is the correct fix?

options.A: Set the environment variable CLAUDE_NONINTERACTIVE=1.
options.B: Pipe the prompt via stdin: echo "Review..." | claude.
options.C: Use the -p flag: claude -p "Review this PR for security issues".
options.D: Add --headless to the command.

questions.explanation: The -p (or --print) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode for automated pipelines. It outputs the result directly to stdout without launching the interactive REPL. The other options reference non-existent features.

questions.question: You are building a data extraction pipeline. Claude outputs JSON that matches your schema structurally but contains fabricated values for fields where the source document had no information. What schema design change would address this?

options.A: Add stricter validation rules that reject any null values.
options.B: Make those fields optional (nullable) in the schema so the model can return null instead of being forced to fabricate values for required fields.
options.C: Add a post-processing step that removes suspicious-looking values.
options.D: Increase the number of few-shot examples to show correct extraction.

questions.explanation: When source documents may not contain certain information, marking those schema fields as optional/nullable prevents the model from fabricating values to satisfy required field constraints. The model can legitimately return null instead of inventing data.

questions.question: Your extraction pipeline retries failed validations by sending 'There were errors in your extraction. Please try again.' The retry rarely fixes the issues. What should you change?

options.A: Increase the retry count from 3 to 10.
options.B: Switch to a more capable model for retries.
options.C: Skip retries and route all failures to human review.
options.D: Append the specific validation errors to the retry prompt (which field failed, what was wrong, expected vs actual value) so the model knows exactly what to fix.

questions.explanation: Generic retry messages provide no signal for correction. Specific error feedback (field name, violation type, expected format) guides the model toward the correct output. This is the same principle as giving specific, actionable feedback rather than 'try again.'

questions.question: Your team uses the Message Batches API for a weekly compliance report (processed overnight). A new requirement adds a blocking pre-commit hook that must analyze code before developers can commit. How should you handle the new workflow?

options.A: Use the synchronous API for the pre-commit hook and continue using the Batches API for the weekly report.
options.B: Add the pre-commit analysis to the existing batch job and increase submission frequency.
options.C: Use the Batches API for both since it saves 50% on costs.
options.D: Switch everything to synchronous API for consistency.

questions.explanation: The Batch API processes requests within up to 24 hours, making it unsuitable for blocking workflows like pre-commit hooks that need immediate results. Use the synchronous API for latency-sensitive tasks and keep the Batch API for latency-tolerant overnight jobs.

questions.question: A customer support agent handles a multi-issue conversation. After 15 turns, the agent references the wrong order number when processing a refund. What context management approach would prevent this?

options.A: Extract critical transactional facts (customer ID, order numbers, amounts, statuses) into an immutable 'case facts' block positioned at the start of each prompt, outside of summarized history.
options.B: Increase the model's max_tokens to accommodate longer conversations.
options.C: Summarize the conversation every 5 turns to keep context manageable.
options.D: Add instructions to the system prompt saying 'Always double-check order numbers before processing.'

questions.explanation: Progressive summarization loses critical details. An immutable 'case facts' block preserves exact values (IDs, amounts, dates) in a high-recall position (beginning of context), ensuring they are never lost or altered during conversation summarization.

questions.question: Your agent's overall extraction accuracy is 96%. After deploying to production, clients report poor results on invoice documents. Investigation shows invoice accuracy is 72% while receipt accuracy is 99%. What metric design flaw allowed this?

options.A: The validation set was too small to be statistically significant.
options.B: The test documents were not representative of production traffic.
options.C: Aggregate accuracy metrics masked per-document-type failures. The solution is to track accuracy stratified by document type.
options.D: The model was overfitting to the most common document type.

questions.explanation: Aggregate metrics can hide poor performance on specific segments. 99% on receipts + 72% on invoices can average to 96% overall if receipts dominate the dataset. Stratified metrics by document type reveal these hidden failures.

questions.question: In a multi-agent research system, the final report states 'AI adoption grew 40% in 2025' without any source. The coordinator cannot determine which subagent produced this claim or where it came from. What architectural change would prevent this?

options.A: Have the coordinator verify every claim by calling a separate fact-checking subagent.
options.B: Require all subagents to output structured claim-source mappings (claim text, source URL, document name, publication date) that downstream agents must preserve through synthesis.
options.C: Add a post-synthesis step that searches the internet to verify all statistics.
options.D: Limit the synthesis agent to only quoting exact text from source documents.

questions.explanation: Information provenance requires structured claim-source mappings from the start. Without them, attribution is irretrievably lost during summarization. Post-hoc verification is unreliable because the original source may no longer be determinable.

questions.task_title: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.tested_concept: The distinction between model-driven decision-making (Claude reasons about which tool to call next based on context) and pre-configured decision trees or tool sequences
questions.question:
Scenario: Autonomous Customer Support Triage
Your team is building an agentic support system that must handle two distinct workflows: (1) processing refund requests, which always follow a strict 4-step compliance sequence (verify purchase, check return window, calculate amount, issue refund), and (2) investigating complex billing disputes, which require the agent to dynamically decide whether to check account history, contact the billing department, or request additional documentation based on the specific details of each case. How should you architect the decision-making logic for these two workflows to best leverage Claude's capabilities while maintaining compliance?

options.A: Implement both workflows as pre-configured decision trees with fixed tool sequences, using Claude only to extract the necessary parameters from the user's message to populate the steps.
options.B: Use a pre-configured decision tree for the refund workflow to ensure strict compliance, but implement a model-driven agentic loop for the billing dispute workflow where Claude reasons about the context to dynamically select the next tool or action.
options.C: Implement both workflows as model-driven agentic loops, relying on Claude's reasoning to determine the correct sequence of steps for both refunds and billing disputes based on the conversation context.
options.D: Use a pre-configured decision tree for the billing dispute workflow to ensure consistent handling, but implement a model-driven agentic loop for the refund workflow to allow Claude to skip unnecessary compliance steps when appropriate.

questions.explanation: Correct answer B correctly applies the distinction between model-driven decision-making and pre-configured sequences. The refund workflow has a strict, deterministic 4-step compliance sequence that must never be skipped or reordered, making a pre-configured decision tree the appropriate choice to guarantee compliance. The billing dispute workflow is complex and variable, requiring the agent to reason about context to decide which investigation path to take, which is exactly what a model-driven agentic loop (where Claude decides the next tool/action) is designed for. Option A is wrong because it treats the complex billing dispute as a fixed sequence, ignoring the need for dynamic reasoning and limiting the agent to a rigid path that cannot adapt to unique dispute details. Option C is wrong because it uses a model-driven loop for the refund workflow, which introduces unnecessary risk of non-compliance since Claude could theoretically hallucinate or skip required compliance steps, whereas a fixed sequence guarantees the correct order. Option D is wrong because it reverses the appropriate architectures: it uses a rigid decision tree for the complex, variable billing dispute (which needs dynamic reasoning) and a model-driven loop for the strict compliance refund process (which needs fixed sequencing), thereby failing to leverage Claude's reasoning where it's needed and risking compliance where it's critical.

questions.task_title: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.tested_concept: Implementing agentic loop control flow that continues when stop_reason is "tool_use" and terminates when stop_reason is "end_turn"
questions.question:
Scenario: Autonomous Data Pipeline
Your team is building a data processing agent that must query a database, transform the results, and then write them to a file. The agent uses a loop to handle multiple tool calls. Currently, the agent executes the database query tool but then stops responding, failing to perform the transformation and file write steps. The logs show the agent's last response had a stop_reason of 'tool_use'. Which loop control logic correctly implements the agentic pattern to ensure the agent continues processing until the task is fully complete?

options.A: Continue the loop and send the tool results back to the model only when stop_reason is 'tool_use'; break the loop and return the final response when stop_reason is 'end_turn'.
options.B: Break the loop immediately after the first tool execution regardless of stop_reason, then manually invoke the next tool in a separate sequential script.
options.C: Continue the loop and send the tool results back to the model only when stop_reason is 'end_turn'; break the loop when stop_reason is 'tool_use'.
options.D: Always break the loop after any tool execution and restart the entire agent session with a new prompt containing the previous results.

questions.explanation: The correct agentic loop pattern requires checking the stop_reason of the model's response. When stop_reason is 'tool_use', the agent must continue the loop by executing the requested tool(s) and sending the results back to the model for further processing. The loop should only terminate (break) when stop_reason is 'end_turn', indicating the model has finished its reasoning and tool usage. Option B fails because it breaks the agentic loop prematurely, preventing the model from orchestrating subsequent steps. Option C inverts the logic, breaking the loop when the model explicitly requests more tool usage and continuing when the model is done, which would cause the agent to hang or fail to execute tools. Option D is inefficient and incorrect because it restarts the entire session rather than continuing the existing conversation context, losing the agentic loop's ability to maintain state across multiple tool calls within a single interaction.

questions.task_title: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.tested_concept: The agentic loop lifecycle: sending requests to Claude, inspecting stop_reason ("tool_use" vs "end_turn"), executing requested tools, and returning results for the next iteration
questions.question:
Scenario: Autonomous Data Pipeline Orchestrator
Your team is building an agentic system that autonomously validates and transforms data files. The system uses a loop where Claude receives a file path, inspects the file, and decides whether to transform it or flag it for review. During testing, you notice that when Claude determines a file needs transformation, it outputs a tool_use block to call the transform function, but the system immediately terminates the workflow instead of executing the tool and continuing. The system currently checks if Claude's response contains any text before deciding to stop. What is the correct implementation pattern to fix this behavior and ensure the agentic loop continues properly?

options.A: Check the stop_reason field in Claude's response; if it is 'tool_use', execute the requested tool, append the result to the conversation history, and send a new request to Claude; if it is 'end_turn', terminate the loop.
options.B: Parse the text content of Claude's response for keywords like 'transform' or 'done'; if 'transform' is found, execute the tool and break the loop; if 'done' is found, terminate the workflow.
options.C: Always execute the last tool_use block found in the response regardless of the stop_reason, then immediately terminate the loop to prevent infinite recursion, relying on the tool's output to signal completion.
options.D: Send the tool execution result back to Claude in the same request without a new API call, using the tool_result parameter to inline the execution, and only terminate when Claude returns an empty response.

questions.explanation: The correct agentic loop lifecycle requires inspecting the stop_reason field in Claude's API response. When stop_reason is 'tool_use', the system must execute the requested tool, add the tool result to the conversation history, and send a new request to Claude to continue the loop. When stop_reason is 'end_turn', the loop should terminate. Option A correctly implements this pattern. Option B is wrong because parsing text for keywords is unreliable and ignores the structured stop_reason signal, which is the definitive indicator of whether Claude wants to use a tool or finish. Option C is wrong because terminating immediately after one tool execution breaks the agentic loop, preventing Claude from performing multi-step reasoning or chaining multiple tools. Option D is wrong because the Claude API does not support inlining tool execution results within the same request; tool results must be sent in a subsequent request as part of the conversation history, requiring a new API call to continue the loop.

questions.task_title: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.tested_concept: Avoiding anti-patterns such as parsing natural language signals to determine loop termination, setting arbitrary iteration caps as the primary stopping mechanism, or checking for assistant text content as a completion indicator
questions.question:
Scenario: Automated Code Review Agent
Your team has built an agentic code review system where a Claude agent iteratively reviews a pull request, identifies issues, and suggests fixes. The current implementation uses a fixed loop that runs exactly 10 iterations regardless of the code complexity, and the agent determines when to stop by checking if its own text output contains the phrase 'Review Complete.' During testing, you observe two problems: simple PRs waste resources running unnecessary iterations, while complex PRs hit the 10-iteration cap before finishing, leaving critical bugs unaddressed. Which approach best resolves these issues by addressing the root anti-patterns in the loop design?

options.A: Replace the fixed iteration cap with a dynamic termination condition based on a structured 'issues_remaining' boolean flag in the agent's tool output, and remove the reliance on parsing natural language text for completion signals.
options.B: Increase the fixed iteration cap to 50 iterations to handle complex PRs, and refine the natural language prompt to ensure the agent consistently outputs the exact phrase 'Review Complete' when finished.
options.C: Implement a retry mechanism that re-runs the entire review loop from scratch if the agent's text output does not contain 'Review Complete' after 10 iterations, adding a counter to track total attempts.
options.D: Add a secondary validation agent that reads the first agent's text output, parses it for completion keywords, and then signals the orchestrator to stop the loop if the keyword is found.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because it directly addresses both anti-patterns identified in the concept: it replaces the arbitrary fixed iteration cap (10 iterations) with a dynamic, state-based termination condition (structured boolean flag), and it eliminates the fragile practice of parsing natural language text ('Review Complete') to determine loop completion. Option B is wrong because it doubles down on both anti-patterns by keeping the arbitrary cap (just larger) and relying on parsing natural language text for termination. Option C is wrong because it still relies on parsing natural language text for the completion signal and adds unnecessary complexity by re-running the entire loop rather than continuing iteratively. Option D is wrong because it still relies on parsing natural language text for completion signals (just moving the parsing to a second agent) and does not address the arbitrary iteration cap issue, adding unnecessary architectural complexity.

questions.task_title: Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
questions.tested_concept: The role of the coordinator in task decomposition, delegation, result aggregation, and deciding which subagents to invoke based on query complexity
questions.question:
Scenario: Multi-Agent Research Pipeline
Your team has built a research assistant system using a coordinator-subagent architecture. The coordinator receives user queries and delegates to specialized subagents (e.g., a web search agent, a code analysis agent, and a data visualization agent). Users report that for simple factual questions like 'What is the capital of France?', the system takes 15 seconds to respond and returns an unnecessarily complex report with charts and code snippets, even though a simple text answer would suffice. The coordinator is currently invoking all three subagents for every query regardless of complexity. What is the most effective way to fix this issue?

options.A: Configure the coordinator to perform task decomposition and complexity assessment before delegation, routing simple factual queries directly to a lightweight text-generation subagent while reserving the full multi-agent pipeline only for complex, multi-step research tasks.
options.B: Increase the maximum token limit for the coordinator's system prompt so it can process all three subagent outputs more efficiently and reduce the overall response time through better parallel processing.
options.C: Replace the coordinator with a single monolithic agent that has access to all tools (web search, code analysis, data visualization) and handles every query end-to-end without any delegation or subagent invocation.
options.D: Add a post-processing step after all subagents complete their work that filters out unnecessary charts and code snippets from the final aggregated response, ensuring only relevant information is shown to the user.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the coordinator's core role in a coordinator-subagent pattern includes task decomposition and deciding which subagents to invoke based on query complexity. By assessing complexity upfront, the coordinator can avoid unnecessary delegation to heavy subagents (code analysis, data visualization) for simple queries, directly addressing the latency and over-engineering problem. Option B is wrong because increasing token limits does not address the root cause of invoking unnecessary subagents; it merely allows the coordinator to handle more output from agents that shouldn't have been called in the first place. Option C is wrong because it abandons the coordinator-subagent architecture entirely, replacing it with a monolithic agent, which defeats the purpose of the multi-agent pattern and loses the benefits of specialized subagents for complex tasks. Option D is wrong because it treats the symptom (unnecessary output) rather than the cause (incorrect delegation); filtering results after all subagents have already run is wasteful and still incurs the 15-second latency from invoking unnecessary agents.

questions.task_title: Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
questions.tested_concept: Risks of overly narrow task decomposition by the coordinator, leading to incomplete coverage of broad research topics
questions.question:
Scenario: Multi-Agent Research Coordinator
Your team has built a multi-agent research system where a coordinator agent decomposes broad user queries into sub-tasks for specialized researcher agents. Users report that when asking for a comprehensive analysis of 'the impact of AI on healthcare,' the final report is fragmented, missing key connections between sub-topics, and often omits entire relevant areas like regulatory frameworks. The coordinator is currently breaking the query down into 15+ highly specific sub-questions (e.g., 'list AI tools for radiology,' 'name AI startups in diagnostics'). What is the most likely root cause of this incomplete coverage?

options.A: The coordinator is over-decomposing the task into overly narrow sub-tasks, causing loss of holistic context and creating blind spots between the siloed sub-questions.
options.B: The researcher agents lack sufficient context window capacity to process the detailed sub-questions, causing them to truncate their responses and miss key information.
options.C: The coordinator is using a sequential execution pattern instead of parallel execution, causing later sub-tasks to timeout before the full research scope can be covered.
options.D: The researcher agents are hallucinating because the sub-questions are too specific, leading them to invent details rather than reporting gaps in their knowledge.

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Overly narrow task decomposition by the coordinator is a known risk in multi-agent orchestration. When a broad topic is broken into too many highly specific, siloed sub-questions, the coordinator loses the holistic view needed to ensure complete coverage, leading to gaps (like missing regulatory frameworks) and fragmented results that lack synthesis. This directly matches the symptom of missing key connections and entire relevant areas. Wrong: B. Context window capacity is not the issue here; the problem is the decomposition strategy, not the agents' ability to process individual sub-questions. Wrong: C. Execution pattern (sequential vs. parallel) affects latency, not the completeness or coverage of the research scope. Wrong: D. Hallucination is a separate issue; the problem described is omission and fragmentation due to poor decomposition, not fabrication of details.

questions.task_title: Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning
questions.tested_concept: Designing coordinator prompts that specify research goals and quality criteria rather than step-by-step procedural instructions, to enable subagent adaptability
questions.question:
Scenario: Multi-Agent Research System
Your team is building a multi-agent system where a coordinator agent delegates research tasks to specialized subagents. The coordinator's current prompt contains detailed step-by-step instructions: 'First, search for X. Then, read the top 3 results. Next, extract the date from each. Finally, summarize.' When the research topic shifts from 'latest Python releases' to 'historical economic trends,' the subagents fail because the step-by-step instructions (e.g., 'extract the date') are irrelevant to the new topic. The coordinator cannot adapt its delegation strategy because it is locked into the rigid procedural steps. What is the most effective way to redesign the coordinator's prompt to enable subagent adaptability across diverse research topics?

options.A: Replace the step-by-step instructions with high-level research goals and quality criteria (e.g., 'Find authoritative sources that explain the key drivers of this topic, ensuring at least three distinct perspectives are represented'), allowing subagents to determine the appropriate search and extraction steps.
options.B: Add conditional logic to the coordinator's prompt that checks the topic category and selects from a predefined set of step-by-step instruction templates for each category.
options.C: Increase the context window size for the coordinator so it can include all possible step-by-step instruction variants for every potential research topic in a single prompt.
options.D: Remove the coordinator entirely and have the main application directly invoke subagents with topic-specific prompts, bypassing the need for a coordinator to adapt delegation strategies.

questions.explanation:
Correct: A. The concept tested is designing coordinator prompts that specify research goals and quality criteria rather than step-by-step procedural instructions. By defining high-level goals (e.g., 'find authoritative sources') and quality criteria (e.g., 'three distinct perspectives'), the coordinator enables subagents to adapt their own search and extraction strategies to the specific topic, rather than being constrained by rigid, topic-specific steps. This approach leverages the subagents' ability to reason about the best way to achieve the stated goals.

Why distractors are wrong:
B is wrong because it still relies on step-by-step procedural instructions (just different ones for each category), which fails to enable true adaptability and requires maintaining a large set of predefined templates for every possible topic category.
C is wrong because simply including all possible step-by-step variants in a single prompt does not solve the core problem; the coordinator would still need to select the right template, and the subagents would still be constrained by rigid procedural steps rather than being able to adapt their approach based on the topic.
D is wrong because it removes the coordinator entirely, which defeats the purpose of having a multi-agent system with a coordinator that can dynamically delegate and adapt. The question asks how to redesign the coordinator's prompt, not how to eliminate the coordinator.

questions.task_title: Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning
questions.tested_concept: Using structured data formats to separate content from metadata when passing context between agents to preserve attribution
questions.question:
Scenario: Multi-Agent Research Pipeline
Your team has built a research pipeline where a Coordinator agent delegates queries to specialized Researcher agents. The Coordinator then synthesizes findings into a final report. During testing, the Coordinator frequently misattributes facts to the wrong source, and the final report lacks clear citations because the Researcher agents return plain text summaries that blend source content with their own analysis. The Coordinator cannot distinguish which sentences came from which source document. What is the most effective architectural change to fix this attribution issue?

options.A: Instruct the Researcher agents to return a structured JSON object containing separate fields for 'content' (the factual findings) and 'metadata' (source URLs, document titles, and confidence scores), allowing the Coordinator to programmatically map facts to their origins.
options.B: Ask the Researcher agents to prefix every sentence in their plain text response with the source URL in brackets, such as '[source.com] This fact is true', so the Coordinator can parse the text to extract citations.
options.C: Have the Coordinator agent re-query the original source documents directly for every fact mentioned in the Researcher agents' summaries to independently verify attribution before writing the final report.
options.D: Configure the Researcher agents to append a single summary paragraph at the end of their response listing all sources used, relying on the Coordinator's natural language understanding to infer which facts came from which source.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because it uses structured data formats (JSON) to explicitly separate content from metadata, which is the specific skill tested. This approach preserves attribution by keeping source information in a distinct, machine-readable field rather than blending it into the text, allowing the Coordinator to reliably map facts to origins without parsing ambiguity. Option B is wrong because embedding citations in plain text (prefixing sentences) mixes content and metadata, making parsing fragile and error-prone; the Coordinator must still perform NLP to extract the source, which is less reliable than structured fields. Option C is wrong because re-querying sources is an inefficient workaround that adds unnecessary latency and API calls rather than fixing the context-passing mechanism between agents. Option D is wrong because appending a summary list relies on the Coordinator's LLM inference to guess which fact came from which source, which is prone to hallucination and misattribution since the content and metadata are not explicitly linked in a structured way.

questions.task_title: Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning
questions.tested_concept: The AgentDefinition configuration including descriptions, system prompts, and tool restrictions for each subagent type
questions.question: Your team is building a multi-agent research system where a coordinator agent delegates tasks to specialized subagents. You need to create a 'DataValidator' subagent that must only use a specific 'validate_schema' tool and must never access the 'write_to_database' tool, even if the coordinator agent has access to it. Additionally, the subagent needs a custom system prompt to enforce strict JSON output formatting. Which configuration approach correctly implements these requirements using AgentDefinition?

options.A: Define the AgentDefinition with a custom system prompt for JSON formatting and explicitly list 'validate_schema' in the allowed_tools array while omitting 'write_to_database', relying on the default deny-all behavior for unlisted tools.
options.B: Pass the 'write_to_database' tool to the subagent but include a negative instruction in the system prompt telling the subagent not to use it, while also providing the custom JSON formatting prompt.
options.C: Create a separate AgentDefinition for the DataValidator that inherits all tools from the coordinator agent by default, then add a custom system prompt that instructs the subagent to ignore the 'write_to_database' tool.
options.D: Configure the AgentDefinition with the custom system prompt and include both 'validate_schema' and 'write_to_database' in the allowed_tools array, using a tool-specific description to discourage usage of 'write_to_database'.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because AgentDefinition allows explicit control over tool access via the allowed_tools array; omitting 'write_to_database' enforces a hard restriction (deny-by-default for unlisted tools), which is the only reliable way to prevent tool misuse. The custom system prompt handles the JSON formatting requirement. Option B is wrong because negative instructions in system prompts are unreliable for security restrictions — the subagent could still invoke 'write_to_database' despite the instruction. Option C is wrong because inheriting all tools from the coordinator means 'write_to_database' remains available, and system prompt instructions alone cannot reliably prevent its use. Option D is wrong because including 'write_to_database' in allowed_tools makes it accessible to the subagent, and tool descriptions are not security controls — they cannot prevent the subagent from using the tool.

questions.task_title: Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning
questions.tested_concept: Fork-based session management for exploring divergent approaches from a shared analysis baseline
questions.question:
Scenario: Divergent Strategy Exploration
Your team is building a multi-agent system that analyzes a complex business problem. The coordinator agent has completed a shared baseline analysis of the problem statement and data. Now, the system needs to explore three completely different strategic approaches (e.g., cost-optimization, speed-optimization, and quality-optimization) in parallel. Each approach requires a long, independent chain-of-thought that should not interfere with the others, but all must start from the exact same baseline analysis context. Which session management strategy should you implement to ensure each strategy is explored independently without cross-contamination of reasoning, while efficiently reusing the shared baseline context?

options.A: Fork the current session into three independent child sessions, each inheriting the baseline analysis context but maintaining separate conversation histories for their respective strategy exploration.
options.B: Create three separate new sessions from scratch, re-prompting each with the full baseline analysis text to ensure they start with identical context.
options.C: Use a single shared session for all three strategies, instructing the model to use distinct tags (e.g., [COST], [SPEED], [QUALITY]) to keep the reasoning for each approach logically separated within the same conversation history.
options.D: Spawn three independent subagents that each receive the baseline analysis as a tool input, but do not maintain any persistent session state, relying entirely on the tool's return value for their final recommendation.

questions.explanation: Correct Answer A is correct because fork-based session management is specifically designed to create independent branches from a shared baseline. Each fork inherits the prior context (the baseline analysis) but maintains its own isolated conversation history, preventing cross-contamination between the divergent strategies while avoiding the redundancy of re-sending the baseline. Option B is wrong because re-prompting from scratch with the full baseline text is inefficient (wasting tokens on repeated context) and does not leverage the session management capability to inherit context; it treats the baseline as static input rather than shared session state. Option C is wrong because using a single shared session with tags does not provide true isolation; the model's reasoning for one strategy could leak into or interfere with the others since they share the same conversation history, violating the requirement for independent exploration. Option D is wrong because it describes a stateless tool invocation rather than session management; it fails to maintain the persistent conversation history needed for a 'long, independent chain-of-thought' and does not utilize the fork mechanism to inherit and extend the baseline context within a session framework.

questions.task_title: Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
questions.tested_concept: Implementing programmatic prerequisites that block downstream tool calls until prerequisite steps have completed (e.g., blocking process_refund until get_customer has returned a verified customer ID)
questions.question:
Scenario: You are building a customer support agent that processes refund requests. The agent has access to two tools: `get_customer` (which verifies a customer ID and returns account details) and `process_refund` (which issues a refund to a verified account). During testing, you notice the agent sometimes calls `process_refund` with a customer ID that was never verified by `get_customer`, leading to errors. You need to enforce a strict workflow where `process_refund` can only be called after `get_customer` has successfully returned a verified customer ID. What is the correct implementation approach?
A) Add a system prompt instruction stating 'You must call get_customer before process_refund' and rely on the model to follow this logical sequence.
B) Implement a programmatic prerequisite check in the orchestration layer that blocks the `process_refund` tool call until the `get_customer` tool has completed and returned a verified customer ID.
C) Configure the `process_refund` tool definition to require a `customer_id` parameter, trusting that the model will only generate this parameter if it has previously called `get_customer`.
D) Use a separate classifier model to analyze the conversation history and predict whether the agent has verified the customer before allowing the `process_refund` tool to be invoked.

options.A: Add a system prompt instruction stating 'You must call get_customer before process_refund' and rely on the model to follow this logical sequence.
options.B: Implement a programmatic prerequisite check in the orchestration layer that blocks the process_refund tool call until the get_customer tool has completed and returned a verified customer ID.
options.C: Configure the process_refund tool definition to require a customer_id parameter, trusting that the model will only generate this parameter if it has previously called get_customer.
options.D: Use a separate classifier model to analyze the conversation history and predict whether the agent has verified the customer before allowing the process_refund tool to be invoked.

questions.explanation: Correct answer B is right because it implements programmatic prerequisites in the orchestration layer, which is the specific concept being tested. This approach enforces the workflow by blocking downstream tool calls (process_refund) until prerequisite steps (get_customer) have completed and returned the necessary verified data, ensuring strict compliance regardless of the model's reasoning. Option A is wrong because relying solely on system prompt instructions is not a programmatic enforcement mechanism; LLMs can and do fail to follow logical sequences, leading to the exact errors described. Option C is wrong because requiring a parameter in the tool definition does not prevent the model from hallucinating or generating an unverified customer_id; it only ensures the parameter is present, not that it was verified by a prior step. Option D is wrong because using a separate classifier model is an over-engineered, probabilistic approach that adds unnecessary complexity and latency when a deterministic programmatic prerequisite check in the orchestration layer is the correct and simpler solution.

questions.task_title: Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
questions.tested_concept: When deterministic compliance is required (e.g., identity verification before financial operations), prompt instructions alone have a non-zero failure rate
questions.question: Scenario: You are architecting a multi-step workflow for a fintech application that processes loan applications. The workflow involves a Claude agent reviewing applicant documents, followed by a deterministic identity verification step, and finally a financial disbursement step. The compliance team mandates that the identity verification step must never proceed unless the applicant's government ID has been cryptographically validated against a trusted database. You notice that in 2% of test cases, the Claude agent skips the verification step and proceeds directly to disbursement, claiming the documents 'look correct' based on visual inspection. What is the correct architectural approach to ensure deterministic compliance?

options.A: Refine the system prompt to explicitly state that skipping identity verification is a critical failure, and increase the temperature to 0 to reduce hallucination rates, relying on the model's improved instruction-following capabilities.
options.B: Implement a deterministic code-based guardrail that validates the identity verification status flag before allowing the workflow to transition to the disbursement step, ensuring the step cannot be bypassed regardless of the LLM's output.
options.C: Add a second Claude agent in a 'reviewer' role that critiques the first agent's decision to skip verification, creating a multi-agent debate loop that forces the first agent to reconsider and perform the verification step.
options.D: Use structured output with a JSON schema that requires an 'identity_verified' boolean field, and rely on the model's ability to correctly populate this field based on its visual analysis of the uploaded documents.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B because the concept states that prompt instructions alone have a non-zero failure rate for deterministic compliance requirements. A deterministic code-based guardrail (a hard constraint outside the LLM) is the only approach that guarantees the verification step cannot be bypassed, as it enforces the rule at the orchestration level rather than relying on the probabilistic nature of the LLM. Option A is incorrect because even with temperature 0 and refined prompts, LLMs can still fail to follow instructions (non-zero failure rate), making it insufficient for critical compliance. Option C is incorrect because adding a second LLM agent introduces another probabilistic component; the reviewer agent could also fail to catch the skip, compounding rather than eliminating the risk. Option D is incorrect because structured output only constrains the format of the response, not the truthfulness or completeness of the underlying reasoning; the model could still hallucinate 'true' for identity_verified without actually performing the cryptographic check.

questions.task_title: Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
questions.tested_concept: Compiling structured handoff summaries when escalating to human agents who lack access to the conversation transcript
questions.question: Scenario: Your company's customer support system uses Claude to handle tier-1 inquiries. When a customer requests a refund exceeding $500, the agent must escalate to a human supervisor. The supervisor's dashboard only displays the final escalation message and does not have access to the full conversation transcript due to data privacy constraints. Currently, supervisors are receiving vague messages like 'Escalating for approval,' forcing them to call customers back for details, which increases resolution time. How should you configure the escalation handoff to resolve this?

options.A: Instruct the agent to append the entire conversation history as a quoted text block to the escalation message, ensuring the supervisor has complete context.
options.B: Configure the agent to generate a structured summary of the conversation—including the customer's issue, relevant account details, and the specific refund amount—before sending the escalation message to the supervisor.
options.C: Add a tool that allows the supervisor to query the conversation transcript directly from the database, enabling them to retrieve the full history on demand.
options.D: Set the agent to automatically process the refund without escalation, using a system prompt that grants it permission to approve refunds up to $1000 to avoid the handoff entirely.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B because the scenario explicitly states that supervisors lack access to the conversation transcript. The concept of 'compiling structured handoff summaries' is the specific mechanism designed to bridge this gap by distilling relevant context into a concise, readable format for the human agent. Option A is incorrect because appending the entire conversation history violates the privacy constraint (supervisors shouldn't see the full raw transcript) and is inefficient, as it forces the supervisor to sift through irrelevant dialogue rather than receiving a curated summary. Option C is incorrect because it proposes a technical workaround (querying the database) that contradicts the stated constraint that supervisors lack access to the transcript; the question asks how to configure the handoff, not how to change the supervisor's dashboard permissions. Option D is incorrect because it bypasses the required escalation workflow entirely, ignoring the business rule that refunds over $500 must be reviewed by a human supervisor.

questions.task_title: Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
questions.tested_concept: The difference between programmatic enforcement (hooks, prerequisite gates) and prompt-based guidance for workflow ordering
questions.question:
Scenario: Compliance-Driven Document Review Pipeline
Your team is building a multi-agent pipeline for processing legal contracts. The workflow requires three steps: (1) Extract key clauses, (2) Check clauses against a regulatory database, and (3) Generate a summary. The compliance team mandates that Step 2 must never execute unless Step 1 has successfully completed and returned valid data. Currently, you are using a single prompt that instructs the agent to 'perform these steps in order,' but testing reveals the agent occasionally skips Step 1 and attempts Step 2 directly, causing failures. What is the most robust architectural change to enforce this dependency?

options.A: Refactor the workflow to use programmatic enforcement by implementing a prerequisite gate that validates the output of Step 1 before allowing the orchestration layer to invoke Step 2.
options.B: Rewrite the system prompt to explicitly state that Step 2 is dependent on Step 1, adding a conditional instruction that the agent must check for the presence of extracted clauses before proceeding.
options.C: Add a new Step 0 that asks the agent to confirm it has completed Step 1, using a self-verification prompt to ensure the agent acknowledges the dependency before moving to Step 2.
options.D: Increase the temperature parameter to zero and add a 'chain of thought' instruction requiring the agent to list all completed steps before attempting any new step, relying on deterministic reasoning to enforce order.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because it uses programmatic enforcement (prerequisite gates) rather than prompt-based guidance. In agentic architecture, relying on prompts to enforce strict workflow ordering is unreliable because LLMs can hallucinate completion or skip steps; programmatic gates ensure Step 2 is never invoked unless Step 1's output is validated by code. Option B is wrong because it relies on prompt-based guidance, which the scenario already shows is insufficient (the agent skips steps despite instructions). Option C is wrong because it adds another prompt-based step (self-verification) rather than enforcing the dependency at the orchestration level, which still leaves room for the agent to lie about completion. Option D is wrong because lowering temperature and adding chain-of-thought are prompt-based techniques that improve consistency but do not guarantee strict enforcement of workflow dependencies, as the model can still hallucinate that a step was completed.

questions.task_title: Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
questions.tested_concept: When deterministic compliance is required (e.g., identity verification before financial operations), prompt instructions alone have a non-zero failure rate
questions.question:
Scenario: Financial Compliance Agent
Your team is building an agentic workflow for a fintech application that processes loan applications. The workflow requires strict identity verification (KYC) before any financial data is accessed or loan terms are generated. The current implementation uses a single Claude agent with a system prompt instructing it to 'always verify identity first and refuse to proceed if verification fails.' During testing, you observe that the agent occasionally skips the verification step or hallucinates a successful verification when the user provides ambiguous input, resulting in a non-zero failure rate for compliance. What is the most effective architectural change to ensure deterministic compliance?

options.A: Refactor the workflow into a multi-agent orchestration pattern where a dedicated verification agent must explicitly approve the request before handing off control to the financial processing agent, enforcing a hard gate.
options.B: Increase the temperature parameter to 0.0 and add a detailed few-shot example section to the system prompt demonstrating correct identity verification behavior for every possible edge case.
options.C: Implement a post-processing step that uses a separate classifier model to scan the agent's final output for any mention of loan terms and reject the response if the verification step was not explicitly logged.
options.D: Add a self-correction loop where the agent reviews its own reasoning trace after generating a response and automatically regenerates the output if it detects that the identity verification step was skipped.

questions.explanation:
Correct Answer A is right because the core concept is that prompt instructions alone have a non-zero failure rate for deterministic compliance. By refactoring into a multi-agent orchestration pattern with a hard gate (handoff pattern), you enforce the verification step structurally rather than relying on the LLM's probabilistic adherence to instructions. The verification agent acts as a mandatory checkpoint; the financial agent cannot execute until the verification agent explicitly approves, making compliance deterministic through architecture rather than prompting.

Distractor B is wrong because it relies entirely on prompt engineering (temperature adjustment and few-shot examples). While this may reduce errors, it does not eliminate the non-zero failure rate inherent to LLMs following instructions, so it fails to meet the requirement for deterministic compliance.

Distractor C is wrong because it is a reactive post-processing check rather than a proactive enforcement mechanism. It scans the final output, but the damage (accessing financial data or generating loan terms) may have already occurred during the agent's internal reasoning or tool use before the final output was generated. It also adds latency and complexity without solving the root cause of the agent skipping steps.

Distractor D is wrong because it relies on the same LLM to self-correct its own failure. If the agent already hallucinated or skipped the verification step, it is unlikely to reliably detect that it skipped it in its own reasoning trace, as the same probabilistic model that made the error is now asked to catch it. This does not provide the deterministic guarantee required for compliance.

questions.task_title: Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization
questions.tested_concept: Implementing PostToolUse hooks to normalize heterogeneous data formats from different MCP tools before the agent processes them
questions.question: Your team is building a multi-agent research system that uses the Agent SDK. The system integrates two MCP tools: 'WeatherAPI' (returns JSON with metric units like Celsius) and 'LegacySensor' (returns XML with imperial units like Fahrenheit). The downstream agent expects a unified JSON object with metric units. Currently, the agent receives raw, heterogeneous outputs and fails to parse the LegacySensor data correctly. Which approach correctly uses Agent SDK hooks to normalize this data before the agent processes it?

options.A: Implement a PostToolUse hook that intercepts the raw output from both tools, converts the LegacySensor XML to JSON and transforms Fahrenheit to Celsius, ensuring the agent receives a consistent metric JSON payload.
options.B: Implement a PreToolUse hook that modifies the input prompt sent to the LegacySensor tool, instructing it to return JSON in metric units instead of XML in imperial units.
options.C: Configure the MCP server for LegacySensor to output JSON directly, and rely on the agent's built-in multimodal parsing capabilities to automatically convert Fahrenheit to Celsius without any SDK hooks.
options.D: Implement a PostToolUse hook that only validates the JSON schema of the WeatherAPI output, while letting the agent handle the XML parsing and unit conversion for LegacySensor through natural language instructions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A because the PostToolUse hook is specifically designed to intercept and transform tool outputs after execution but before the agent processes them. This is the ideal place to normalize heterogeneous data formats (XML vs JSON) and units (Fahrenheit vs Celsius) into a consistent structure the agent expects. Option B is wrong because PreToolUse hooks modify inputs/prompts before the tool runs; they cannot change the LegacySensor's hardcoded XML output format or unit system, which is an external MCP tool behavior. Option C is wrong because it assumes the MCP server can be reconfigured (which may not be possible for a third-party tool) and incorrectly relies on the agent's multimodal parsing to handle structural normalization and unit conversion, which is unreliable and not the purpose of the Agent SDK hooks. Option D is wrong because it only validates one tool's output and leaves the heterogeneous XML data unnormalized, forcing the agent to handle parsing and conversion itself, which defeats the purpose of using a PostToolUse hook for data normalization.

questions.task_title: Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization
questions.tested_concept: The distinction between using hooks for deterministic guarantees versus relying on prompt instructions for probabilistic compliance
questions.question:
Scenario: Financial Compliance Data Extraction
Your team is building a financial reporting agent that extracts transaction data from unstructured PDF invoices. The downstream accounting system requires strict ISO 8601 date formatting (YYYY-MM-DD) and exact currency codes (e.g., USD, EUR) to function correctly. The current implementation uses prompt instructions asking Claude to 'format dates as ISO 8601 and use standard currency codes,' but the system occasionally fails because Claude sometimes outputs dates like '2024-1-5' (missing leading zeros) or uses full currency names like 'US Dollar' instead of 'USD.' You need to guarantee 100% compliance with these formats to prevent downstream processing errors. What is the most appropriate architectural change?

options.A: Refine the system prompt to include explicit examples of correct and incorrect date/currency formats, relying on few-shot prompting to improve Claude's probabilistic adherence to the instructions.
options.B: Implement Agent SDK hooks to intercept tool calls and programmatically normalize the extracted date and currency values into the required ISO 8601 and standard code formats before passing them to the downstream system.
options.C: Switch to a smaller, more deterministic model variant that is less likely to hallucinate or deviate from formatting instructions, accepting a potential trade-off in extraction accuracy for higher format compliance.
options.D: Add a validation step in the downstream accounting system that rejects non-compliant formats and returns an error message to Claude, instructing it to retry the extraction with corrected formatting.

questions.explanation: Correct Answer B is right because Agent SDK hooks provide deterministic guarantees by programmatically intercepting and normalizing data before it reaches the downstream system, ensuring 100% compliance with strict format requirements regardless of the model's probabilistic output. Option A is wrong because prompt instructions (even with few-shot examples) only provide probabilistic compliance — Claude may still occasionally output non-conforming formats like '2024-1-5' or 'US Dollar,' which would cause downstream failures. Option C is wrong because switching to a smaller model doesn't guarantee deterministic format compliance and would likely reduce extraction accuracy, trading one problem for another without solving the root issue. Option D is wrong because while retry loops can improve compliance, they still rely on Claude's probabilistic ability to correct itself on retry and don't provide the deterministic guarantee that programmatic normalization via hooks does — Claude could still fail to correct the format on subsequent attempts, creating an unreliable system.

questions.task_title: Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization
